commands.meaning: Shows software version, uptime, model, serial details, image, and configuration register.
good_output: Configuration register is normal for the platform

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The device may be set to ignore startup configuration at boot.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: Configuration register 0x2142 is commonly used during password recovery and can cause the device to boot without loading saved configuration.
possible_causes: Configuration register was changed and not restored
Device was staged or recovered recently
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not reload the device until the configuration register and saved configuration state are confirmed.

next_commands.why: Checks the active configuration currently running in memory.
next_commands.meaning: Displays the current configuration applied to the device.
look_for_good: Expected hostname, interfaces, VLANs, and routing settings

next_commands.why: Checks the saved configuration that would load after reboot.
next_commands.meaning: Displays the configuration saved in NVRAM.
look_for_good: Saved configuration matches expected production state
look_for_bad: Startup configuration differs from running configuration

commands.learning_note: Version output is useful context before deeper troubleshooting because platform and software differences affect command behavior.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows switch port status, VLAN, duplex, speed, and media type.
use_for: Check if a port is connected

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The switch port appears to be err-disabled.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The switch has automatically disabled the interface after detecting a protective condition.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not re-enable the port until the err-disable cause is identified in logs and the connected device/path is checked.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The switch port appears to be physically down.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The switch does not detect an active endpoint or cable on this port.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not change VLAN or reboot the switch until physical link and MAC learning are confirmed.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The switch port is administratively disabled or unavailable.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The port is not forwarding user traffic in its current state.
possible_causes: Template or policy disabled the port
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not enable the port without confirming ownership, endpoint, VLAN, and change approval.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The switch port appears physically connected.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The switch detects link on at least one interface in the pasted output.
possible_causes: No physical fault is visible in the status output
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not change the interface until counters, VLAN, and MAC learning are checked if users still report an issue.

next_commands.why: Checks detailed port status, errors, speed, duplex, and counters.
next_commands.meaning: Shows the physical and protocol state plus traffic and error counters for the interface.

next_commands.why: Checks port configuration.
next_commands.meaning: Displays the configuration applied only to the selected interface.

next_commands.why: Checks if the switch has learned a MAC address from the connected device.
next_commands.meaning: Shows MAC addresses learned on the selected interface.
look_for_good: MAC address is in the expected VLAN
look_for_bad: MAC address appears in the wrong VLAN

commands.learning_note: Interface status helps confirm whether the problem is physical, VLAN-related, or configuration-related.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows detailed interface state, counters, errors, speed, duplex, and traffic.
good_output: is up, line protocol is up
bad_output: is down, line protocol is down
normal_patterns: is up, line protocol is up
warning_patterns: is down, line protocol is down

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The interface is administratively down.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The interface has been disabled by configuration and will not pass traffic until it is intentionally enabled.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not enable the port without confirming ownership, endpoint, VLAN, and approval.

patterns: is down, line protocol is down
diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The interface is down at both physical and protocol layers.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The device is not seeing a usable link on the interface.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not change VLAN or trunk settings until physical cabling and endpoint state are confirmed.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The interface shows physical-layer error indicators.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: CRC or input errors often point to cabling, optic, speed, duplex, or hardware issues.
possible_causes: Bad copper cable or fiber optic
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not reset counters as a first step if evidence is needed for escalation.

patterns: is up, line protocol is up
diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The interface appears operational.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The physical and protocol layers are both up in the pasted output.
possible_causes: No link-state fault is visible in this output
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not make configuration changes if the issue may be above Layer 1 or Layer 2.

next_commands.why: Compares detailed interface state against the switchport summary.
next_commands.meaning: Shows status, VLAN, duplex, speed, and media for switchports.

next_commands.why: Checks whether configuration explains the interface behavior.
next_commands.meaning: Displays configuration applied to the selected interface.

next_commands.why: Checks whether frames are being learned from the endpoint.
next_commands.meaning: Lists learned MAC addresses on the selected interface.

commands.learning_note: Interface counters help separate physical faults from configuration or endpoint issues.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows VLAN IDs, VLAN names, status, and access ports assigned to each VLAN.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: A VLAN appears suspended or locally shut.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: Ports in that VLAN may not pass traffic as expected.
possible_causes: VTP or platform policy changed VLAN state
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not add or remove VLANs on trunks until VLAN ownership and path are confirmed.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The pasted VLAN output contains active VLANs.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: At least one VLAN is present and active in the pasted output.
possible_causes: No VLAN state issue is visible from this output alone
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not change access VLANs without confirming endpoint requirements.

next_commands.why: Checks the access VLAN assigned to each switchport.
next_commands.meaning: Shows port state and VLAN summary.

next_commands.why: Checks whether devices are learning MAC addresses in the VLAN.
next_commands.meaning: Lists MAC addresses learned in a specific VLAN.
look_for_good: Expected endpoint MAC addresses are present

commands.learning_note: VLAN output confirms existence and access-port placement, but trunk allowance may need separate checks.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows MAC addresses learned by the switch and the interfaces where they were learned.
use_for: Find where a device is connected

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: No matching MAC address entry is visible.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The switch may not be learning frames from the endpoint or VLAN path being checked.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not move VLANs or patching until link state and endpoint power are checked.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The switch is learning at least one dynamic MAC address.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: MAC learning indicates Layer 2 traffic is reaching the switch.
possible_causes: No MAC-learning fault is visible in this output
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not clear the MAC table unless directed during a controlled troubleshooting step.

next_commands.why: Checks whether the interface learning the MAC has errors or instability.
next_commands.meaning: Shows physical state and counters.

next_commands.why: Confirms the VLAN associated with the learned MAC exists and is active.
next_commands.meaning: Shows VLAN status and access-port membership.

commands.learning_note: A MAC address usually appears only after the endpoint sends traffic.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows Cisco Discovery Protocol neighbors visible from local interfaces.
use_for: Check local and remote interface mapping

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: No CDP neighbor is visible in the pasted output.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The connected device may not support CDP, CDP may be disabled, or no neighbor is connected.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not enable discovery protocols without confirming site policy.

next_commands.why: Checks for standards-based LLDP neighbors.
next_commands.meaning: Lists neighbors discovered by LLDP.

next_commands.why: Checks whether the local interface has link.
next_commands.meaning: Shows switchport link and VLAN state.

commands.learning_note: CDP is Cisco-specific; LLDP is better for mixed-vendor neighbor checks.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows LLDP neighbors visible from local interfaces.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: No LLDP neighbor is visible in the pasted output.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The neighbor may not send LLDP, LLDP may be disabled, or the physical link may be down.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not enable LLDP without confirming site policy.

next_commands.why: Checks physical port state.
next_commands.meaning: Shows link, VLAN, speed, and duplex summary.

next_commands.why: Checks whether a Cisco neighbor is visible through CDP.
next_commands.meaning: Lists CDP neighbors.

commands.learning_note: LLDP is useful for mixed-vendor topology discovery but may be disabled by policy.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows IP-to-MAC address mappings learned by ARP.
good_output: Expected IP has a MAC address

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: ARP resolution is incomplete for at least one IP address.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The device has not resolved the IP address to a MAC address.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not change routes or VLANs until IP, mask, gateway, and Layer 2 path are confirmed.

next_commands.why: Checks Layer 2 learning in the VLAN where ARP is expected.
next_commands.meaning: Lists learned MAC addresses for the VLAN.

next_commands.why: Checks interface state and errors on the path.
next_commands.meaning: Shows physical/protocol state and counters.

commands.learning_note: ARP proves local subnet reachability only after the endpoint or gateway responds at Layer 2.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows spanning-tree state, root bridge information, and port roles.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: A spanning-tree port is blocking.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: Blocking can be normal loop prevention, but unexpected blocking can break a path.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not force ports forwarding or change spanning-tree priority without approval.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: Spanning-tree reports an inconsistent state.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The switch may be protecting the network from a dangerous topology or mismatch.
possible_causes: BPDU guard or root guard event
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not disable spanning-tree protections until the topology and connected device are verified.

next_commands.why: Maps spanning-tree ports to current link status.
next_commands.meaning: Shows port status, VLAN, speed, and duplex.

next_commands.why: Checks physical neighbors that may explain topology.
next_commands.meaning: Lists Cisco neighbors by local interface.

commands.learning_note: Spanning-tree blocking is often normal; the key question is whether the blocking state matches the intended topology.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows port-channel groups, protocol, and member-port bundle state.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: A port-channel member may not be bundled correctly.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: A member link outside the bundle can reduce redundancy or indicate a configuration mismatch.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not remove or add port-channel members until both ends and the change window are confirmed.

next_commands.why: Checks the member interface for physical errors or link state.
next_commands.meaning: Shows detailed state and counters.

next_commands.why: Checks port-channel membership and trunk settings.
next_commands.meaning: Shows interface configuration.

commands.learning_note: Port-channel problems often require comparing both ends; one-sided output rarely proves the full cause.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.

commands.meaning: Shows the active configuration applied to a specific interface.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: The interface configuration contains shutdown.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: The interface may be intentionally disabled by configuration.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not remove shutdown without confirming endpoint ownership, VLAN, and approval.

diagnosis_rules.diagnosis: Port security is configured on the interface.
diagnosis_rules.meaning: Traffic may be restricted based on learned or configured MAC address policy.
diagnosis_rules.do_not_touch: Do not clear or change port-security settings without checking policy and logs.

next_commands.why: Checks whether the configuration matches the actual port state.
next_commands.meaning: Shows link, VLAN, duplex, speed, and media summary.

next_commands.why: Checks physical state and counters for the configured interface.
next_commands.meaning: Shows detailed state and counters.

commands.learning_note: Running-config explains intended behavior; interface status and counters show current behavior.
commands.source_note: Local command knowledge base. Verify exact command behavior against vendor documentation for platform-specific differences.
